1.0. INTRODUCTION
1.1. On 8th September 2022, the High Court for Zambia, sitting at
Lusaka, dismissed the applicant's petition for the winding-up of
the respondent company.
1.2. The applicant was unhappy with the High Court judgment and
thus appealed to the Court of Appeal. On 15th March 2024 the
Court of Appeal dismissed the applicant's appeal. The applicant
thereupon sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. That
R3
application was unsuccessful as in its ruling of 31st October
2024, the Court of Appeal opined that the intended appeal did
not meet the threshold set out in section 13 of the Court of
Appeal Act for the grant of leave to appeal.
1.3. Undeterred by that development, the applicant renewed the
application for leave to appeal before a single judge of this court
who, in a ruling delivered on 11th December 2024, equally
dismissed the application. The applicant has now relaunched
the application before the full court.
2.0. BACKGROUND
2.1. In a petition filed in the High Court in June 2024 pursuant to
section 57(l)(b) of the Corporate Insolvency Act, No. 9 of 2017
the applicant sought a court order for the liquidation of the
respondent company on account of its failure to pay debts.
2.2. Going back in time, the applicant and the respondent company
had concluded a supply agreement whereby the applicant, as
supplier, was to make available to the respondent, equipment,
software and services to enable the respondent fulfil its
obligations under a different agreement with a third party.
R4
2.3. The applicant performed its side of the bargain and tendered
two separate invoices for the supplied "works." There were
arithmetical differences regarding the bottom-line figure
claimed by the applicant as part of the equipment covered in
the invoices had not been utilised and the applicant sought to
retrieve that equipment. The monetary value, of that equipment
was to be deducted from the total invoice sum.
2.4. When demand for what was supposedly due and owing was
made by the applicant to the respondent, the latter, in response,
readily accepted that it was in financial distress following the
cancellation of its concession agreement with the Government
of Zambia and was thus unable to meet its debt obligations. It
was sequel to these developments that the winding-up petition
was filed.
2.5. The Interested Party joined the proceedings through a notice of
intention to appear filed after the presentation of the petition.
It claims that as a shareholder in the applicant company, it had
a legitimate interest to be heard in the winding-up petition.
RS
2.6. The learned High Court judge dismissed the winding-up petition
on, among other grounds, the finding he made that the
petitioner was not a clear-cut creditor as envisaged in the law
respecting winding-up petitions. In any case, the Interested
Party had raised sufficient doubt in the judge's mind as to the
petitioner's indebtedness. The judge was of the view that 'the
debt had been disputed on substantial grounds.'
2.7. The High Court judge also held that the reliance by the
petitioner in the winding-up petition on the Companies
(Winding-up) Rules 1929 was wrong as the correct Rules to be
invoked were the Companies (Winding-up) Rules 2004.
2.8. The applicant appealed the High Court decision to the Court of
Appeal. When the Court of Appeal delivered its judgment on
15th March 2024, the grief of the applicant was heightened as
the Court of Appeal upheld the decision of the High Court.
2.9. The applicant continued to grumble that the High Court and the
Court of Appeal had each in turn missed the point or misapplied
the law. The applicant thus sought to appeal to the Supreme
Court. To do so, it required the permission of the Court of
R6
Appeal in terms of section 13 of the Court Appeal Act and Rule
51 of the Supreme Court Rules Chapter 25 of the Laws of
Zambia. The application for permission to appeal fell on rocky
ground as it was rejected by the Court of Appeal in its ruling of
30th October 2024.
2.10. In declining leave to appeal, the Court of Appeal reasoned that
the intended appeal did not raise any point of law of public
importance as both grounds "raise issues affecting only the
parties to the case and not the general public," and that the
applicant had failed to show that "there are real prospects of
success of the intended appeal."
2.11. In view of the foregoing developments, the applicant launched a
renewed application before a single judge of this court in
keeping with section 25A of the Supreme Court Act, Chapter 25
of the Laws of Zambia.
2.12. As already intimated, the single judge upheld the decision of the
Court of Appeal, holding that he found no merit in the
application for leave to appeal. He accordingly declined to grant
permission to appeal to the applicant.
R7
2.13. By way of escalated renewal of the application for leave to
appeal, the applicant has now approached the full court.
3.0. THE APPLICANT'S CASE
3.1. At the hearing of the motion, Mrs. Namwila, learned counsel for
the applicant relied on the documents already filed and more
particularly the skeleton arguments.
3.2. In the skeleton arguments, initially, issues were identified for
determination as follows:
(a) Whether this court has jurisdiction to hear and determine
this application;
(b) Whether the appellant has demonstrated sufficient
reasons to be granted leave to appeal; and
(c) Whether the appeal has a reasonable prospect of success.
3.3. The first of the three issues was procedural in substance,
impeaching the trial court's decision that it could determine on
its own motion that the petition was irregularly before it in the
absence of a formal application by the interested party.
R8
3.4. It would appear that when the notice of motion was amended
and refiled, the first of the three proposed grounds of appeal
was abandoned, leaving the two.
3.5. What emerges after making spirited arguments around the
proposed two grounds of the app
